prevalence.source: Orphanet; Indian IEM center reports and registry syntheses
prevalence.trend: stable; increased diagnosis with improved metabolic testing capacity

incidence.source: IEM incidence syntheses; tertiary center case series

burden.dalyPerCapita: very low (rare), but high per-patient burden (recurrent metabolic crises, neurodevelopmental injury)
burden.mortalityRate: Higher without early diagnosis and emergency protocols

riskFactors: dietary protein load in infancy without management
vulnerablePopulations: patients in areas without metabolic specialists
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Elevated C3 on MS/MS, metabolic acidosis and hyperammonemia, urine organic acids (methylcitrate, 3-hydroxypropionate), confirmatory genetics/enzymes

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: limited and mostly private/tertiary programs; not universal
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Variable; improved with consistent diet/medication and emergency management plans

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Often diagnosed after severe neonatal/infant presentation; confirmatory testing centralized
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Emergency metabolic crisis protocol, protein restriction with formulas, carnitine, intermittent gut decontamination antibiotics, cardiology/neuro follow-up
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Newborn screening gaps, cost of medical nutrition, limited metabolic ICU protocols outside tertiary centers
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Tertiary pediatric hospitals increasingly equipped for IEM diagnosis and emergency care
